title: Living in Boulder vlog 1 - 12 - 2018: Google Earthing Roman Coloseum

enrichment.summary: Kyle recounts excitedly discovering the 'Rome Reborn' 3D model layer in Google Earth after a late night of work, then walks through its limitations (floating buildings, no ground-level walking, slow loading, all-white Colosseum) compared to his own ongoing ancient Rome reconstruction project in a simpler 3D program. He explains why he still sees value in continuing his own version and shares broader ambitions to map colonized Roman towns using a book on Roman agrarian land laws.
enrichment.speaker: kyle
topics: Roman history and republic vs empire
Roman colonial towns and land laws

stances.position: Impressive achievement but too limited for his use case — buildings float, can't be downloaded, no interior access, poor performance
Still worth continuing because it offers transparency, interior views, color-coded data, and works across all scales from aerial to ground level
The Republic era is the most interesting — more democratic, expansionist, exciting history; the Empire is boring and corrupt
Should be $70 not $700; Google should give it to schools and kids to crowdsource historical reconstructions
They did a good job but lack the awning/ropes on the Colosseum and don't take advantage of terrain contours

entities: Roman Agrarian Land Laws of the Republic (book)

claims.claim: I downloaded the model of ancient Rome from Google Earth.

claims.subject: Kyle

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: The Coliseum in the model was all white and lacked detail.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I want to create my own version of ancient Rome with more detail.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I want to make my version of ancient Rome color-coded and organized by building type.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: The Google Earth model doesn't account for the contours of the land.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I find the early history of Rome more interesting than the empire period.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I want to recreate ancient Roman towns using data from a book about Roman land laws.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I made progress on my project, adding ropes to the Colosseum model.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: The Google Earth version of the Colosseum doesn't have an awning or ropes.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I downloaded SketchUp to try it out.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: SketchUp costs $700, which is too expensive for me.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I think SketchUp should be cheaper to make it accessible to more people.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I listened to Aristotle's Politics while working on my project.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I don't need anything more than what I have to continue my project.

transcript.text: Hello, Living in Boulder, V-Log. It's 12.31 a.m. on January 13th.
I'm doing the video earlier today because today I'm more interested in doing a video.
Because yesterday I had quite an interesting day.
I discovered Google Earth.
I mean, I discovered ancient Rome on Google Earth.
Yeah, so last night I'm working, and I worked until like 6 in the morning or 7 in the morning.
And I lied down in bed and had my cell phone with me.
And I was thinking, I wanted to look at the SketchUp, which is this program that makes three-dimensional buildings on Google Earth.
Because I'm just using the Google program, the Polygon tool.
I'm not using the fancy program to make the buildings look realistic.
And I saw a little bit, a couple minutes of a tutorial.
I had like an hour-long tutorial on their YouTube channel, which has 200,000 subscribers.
And so I clicked SketchUp on YouTube.
I saw this video, and it's this chick.

Look at what we did.
Look at what you can do with Google.

And so they show the nice, fancy building in the drawing, and then they show her sitting in front of the real one.
They do like 5-0 here in Boulder.
And then she says something about, yeah, get your, you know, make a building and submit it.
And maybe, I think there may have been a mention about Rome.
Yeah, there was some mention about Google Earth of Rome.

No, it wasn't that video.
It was another video.
I saw two videos.
And they're talking about the Google Earth thing of Rome.

Someone goes, yeah, it would be great if it actually existed.
And I'm like, yeah.
And then I look, and I saw that it was me who made the comment.
And then I looked over to the right, and it was made two years ago.
So I saw that video two years ago.
I looked all over Google, and it wasn't there.
But then I saw below my comment, somebody made a comment to me saying,
All you have to do is go to the Rome Reborn Google Earth
And click on the icon on the thing, and you can download the model.
And I was like, what?
What?
So I jumped out of bed.
You know, it was like 7 in the morning.
I just got in bed.
To get back on the computer.
And, yeah, and I opened up my Rome Reborn.
I clicked on the thing.
I went down to the lower left, and I clicked on it.
There was this option that said 5,000-plus buildings, and I clicked on it.
And then over here on the left, I saw the option, ancient Rome,
Just like in the video.
So I must have tried it two years ago, and it didn't work
Because I don't picture how I wouldn't have seen that there,
And I clicked on it.
But, yeah, I click on it.
I see ancient Rome there on the left, just like with 3D buildings, you know.
And then I start to see a bunch of, like, white dots appear.
And I noticed it's the whole Rome.
They really did Google it all over Rome.
But at that point, I just wanted to go to sleep.
So I went to bed.
I got up the next morning.
I remembered it, and I wondered what.
And I looked, and it had loaded by then.
And, yeah, they rebuilt Rome with very high quality,
With that sketch-up program where it's the red roofs and the tiles
And the walls that look like, you know, stone walls
And the windows and doors and everything and sloped buildings.
But it was all loaded, you know, and it looked great.
And so I had even had the thought before, like, oh, my God, what was I doing?
Have I been wasting my time this whole time?
Because they already had it.
But I wanted to do, you know, what I do with my own Google Earth stuff.
Is zoom in and out really quick and look at the different angles
And all that kind of stuff.
But it doesn't work that way because you can't download the files.
I didn't see anywhere you can download the files.
I'm actually going to do a really thorough search
Because I haven't done a really thorough search
To make sure you can't actually download the files through your computer,
But I don't think you can.
And the way that they did it, because I clicked, it said download or whatever,
And it just opened it up.
But you can't download it, meaning all the information
Of all the buildings, the 5,000-plus buildings or whatever.
It's dependent on how fast your Internet is.
That's why the buildings didn't appear.
So as soon as I, like, if I'd look at a building and I'd zoom out,
You would zoom out to nothing,
And then it would be just a bunch of white dots,

And if you want to go back to the building
And look off in the distance,
It wouldn't work because none of them would be loaded.
And then there was another problem.

Is hovering, like, 300 feet above the real Earth.

And see if those do the same thing.
Yeah, so anyways, at the end of the day, I'm glad that I'm doing what I'm doing.
But they have Rome from 350.

It's really cool to know that they have that, though,
Because from now on, I can reference it with the Google Earth version of Rome.
I was looking at a few buildings that I knew about.
The warehouse, this giant warehouse near Montistachio in the southwestern part of the city.

Like a whole year, is as I can, in the style that I make it,
I can also make Rome in all of the different epochs and the growth of Rome.
Rome in its most interesting is, doesn't even look like how we're used to looking at Rome.
The Rome that we know, that's the giant metropolis Rome, that's the empire of the world
And decadent leeching off of everybody in the world and is boring and controlled by, you know,

It was much smaller.
You know, they weren't even, and you can see it to grow.
Livy describes each different part of it growing.
You know exactly when the part across the river started.
At the end of the kingdom was when the island started.
So it's, that's what I find interesting.
I don't need to make these realistic looking buildings.
I just need to know where they were.
And there's another thing that I want to do with Google Earth with Livy.
I noticed there's this book called, that I got on Kindle,
Called Roman Agrarian Land Laws of the Republic.
And he listed all of the different towns that Rome founded and took over and colonized.
And it listed the land, how much land it was and how many citizens and how many lots.

And how big they were.
And how many of them there were.
You know, how many of them there were and how big they were.

So then you just recreate the city.

And there were like, what, 50 of them?

And, yeah, and then big estates also.
